These ones aren't food grade right? Just keep that in mind wherever we use them. They can't go somewhere where we have a risk of it getting into product
 
Let's get them moved regardless though. One less thing for our visitors to ask questions about today.
I definitely noticed those totes, I did not consider  them because they are not food grade. I won't use that on molding line.

the molds to wash them.

We are removing from the line the molds where the chocolate did not demold. We verified the startup checklist and found that it was not completed this morning. It is important to complete and verify the startup checklist when the line starts to help prevent issues like this from happening.

Weight in the range 



The appearance is good, it has no holes, the bar is not broken and is solid, it also has more than d2 pieces of almond

Please work on plant standards and treat this as though the focus is our plant.

Hi team. I received a call from the Sollich Kitchen 2 operator, who reported that the vacuum pulling the caramel into the kettle wasn't working. I told him to check the filter, and it was dirty. It is crucial that when you wash the kettles, you also wash the filters. Thanks👇

While inspecting the belts, we observed that the belt on Line C-20 showed signs of fraying threads and lint—material from the belt itself—that had not yet come into contact with the product. The supervisor has been notified to take immediate action and prevent further wear on the belt.

The operator at the time of checking the entire line realizes that in the return band 3 there was a white dust on the floor and informs the supervisor and they proceed to call QA to check and we realize that the band is running to one side and is taking with the metal doing so the band will wear out on one side, keeping it reached the area and corrected the band to avoid more wear, the dust found on the floor is band particles and marshmallow.

The product is not compromised because the particles fell to the floor.

As an action, the operators will check the band every 30 minutes to prevent it from happening again

Curt Willis, Justin Garner, Dakota Adair, Jody Howse, Erandy Garcilazo Ceja, Jorel Gourdin, Ira Robison, Italu Martinez, Matt Hatch, Marco Rodriguez, Marcos Jimenez
 
Today we were able to cook a lot of caramel and saw improvement on the Twist Wrap machine. We still have some items Kitchen 4 needs support on, and Theegarten’s programmer will arrive tomorrow to help improve the line. We are now done testing for the day. Todays actions and updates will be added to the L1 Action Tracker
 
Tomorrow’s Plan:
• 8:30 AM – Start cooking Bequet caramel
• Volker (Chocotech) will focus on cooking Bequet caramel and troubleshooting the Conbar hopper filling settings
• Jakob (Sollich) will focus on cooling tunnel settings for the Bequet caramel
• Cornell (Theegarten) and the programmer will adjust line settings to reduce rejects on the Twist Wrap machine

Has the batch amount been decided or will it be determined tomorrow? At some point ingredients will have to be assessed because I thought there was only enough for 12 batches (at 500lbs/batch) and last I heard, these batches will have to be more than that.

Good afternoon Daniel Bringas,

You, Curt Willis, and I should discuss.

I need further clarification regarding the standards we should be using to run our products. This is the first time I have encountered a situation where I am required to adjust the standard based on actual running performance; the sales team does not plan based on the average output of recent production runs. It was previously established that the Matrix machines had a minimum operating speed of 30 pouches per minute; however, according to Matt, these machines are capable of running at 35 or 40 pouches per minute. This is why we are currently using a combined rate of 81 pouches per minute across the three Matrix machines (averaging 27 pouches per minute per machine).
If we have three Matrix machines available, should I only be planning for two? Chad Stone
